content.text: Managing AI risk at enterprise scale begins with a shared vocabulary: a taxonomy that classifies every distinct failure mode, misuse scenario, and compliance exposure into named, measurable categories, and a register that tracks each identified risk through assessment, treatment, and ongoing monitoring. Without a taxonomy, risk language fragments across teams; without a register, identified risks evaporate after the meeting that surfaced them.

attrs.alt: AI Risk Taxonomy and Enterprise Risk Register architecture: taxonomy categories feed into central register, which drives the risk management lifecycle

content.text: What It Is

An AI risk taxonomy is a structured classification system that organises every type of risk associated with AI systems into named, hierarchical categories. For agentic AI — systems that autonomously plan, call tools, and act — the taxonomy must capture risks that go beyond traditional software: model-level failures such as hallucination and value misalignment, emergent agent behaviours, third-party model dependencies, and AI-specific regulatory obligations under frameworks such as the EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF.

An enterprise risk register is the operational artefact that instantiates the taxonomy: each row is a specific, identified risk with a unique identifier, a classification drawn from the taxonomy, a likelihood and impact score, a risk owner, and a current treatment status. Together, the taxonomy and register form the backbone of a formal AI risk programme, enabling consistent communication between engineering, legal, compliance, and executive teams.

The taxonomy is not a one-time artefact — it evolves as new AI capabilities emerge. A 2022 taxonomy that predated function-calling agents lacked entries for tool misuse, excessive delegation, and recursive prompt injection. Maintaining the taxonomy as a living document, versioned and reviewed at least annually, is as important as the initial authoring.
Why It Matters

Without a shared taxonomy, different parts of an organisation describe the same risk in incompatible terms. Legal calls it a 'data protection risk', engineering calls it 'PII leakage in model context', and the CISO's team calls it 'LLM prompt exfiltration'. All three are describing the same failure mode, but without a common name, they are tracked in three separate registers, assigned to three separate owners, and treated in inconsistent and possibly contradictory ways.

Regulators increasingly expect AI risk programmes to demonstrate structured risk identification. The EU AI Act Article 9 mandates a risk management system for high-risk AI applications; NIST AI RMF maps and measures functions require documented risk identification processes. A published taxonomy with a populated register is the evidence artefact that satisfies these requirements. Organisations that cannot produce a coherent taxonomy in an audit face findings of 'inadequate risk governance' — a category that attracts significant remediation effort and reputational cost.

For teams building agentic AI systems specifically, the risk surface is broader than for static ML models. Agents make decisions, call external APIs, write to storage systems, and interact with users in real time. A hallucination in a summarisation tool is a nuisance; a hallucination in an agentic procurement system that approves purchase orders is a material financial risk. The taxonomy must distinguish these categories and the register must weight them accordingly.
Benefits
Shared language
— A taxonomy creates a single controlled vocabulary that engineering, compliance, legal, and the board all use, eliminating miscommunication during incident response and audit.
Complete risk coverage

— A systematically designed taxonomy with required completeness properties prevents blind spots: if the taxonomy has an 'adversarial input' category, teams are forced to consider it even for internal tools.
Audit readiness
— A versioned taxonomy plus a populated, current register is the primary evidence artefact for AI risk audits under ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, and SOC 2 AI addendums.
Prioritisation
— A register with scored risks makes resource allocation decisions transparent: the organisation can defend why it invested in hallucination guardrails before prompt injection mitigations.
Regulatory alignment
— Taxonomies built with reference to NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act Annex III, and ISO 42001 Annex A are easier to cross-reference against regulatory requirements, simplifying compliance gap analysis.
Continuous improvement
— New risks discovered during incidents, threat intelligence, or model upgrades can be systematically added to the taxonomy and immediately added to the register, creating a flywheel of learning.
Trade-offs

A taxonomy adds classification overhead to every risk identification activity. Teams must learn the taxonomy, apply it consistently, and resolve disagreements about which category a given risk falls into. In organisations with limited risk management maturity, the taxonomy itself becomes a source of confusion rather than clarity, especially if it is over-engineered with too many sub-categories.

Large risk registers become difficult to maintain when there are hundreds of entries. Without automated tooling to flag stale entries, review reminders, and ownership changes, registers decay: risks remain in 'Mitigating' status for years after they were resolved, or worse, risks are marked 'Accepted' by owners who have since left the organisation. The register must be treated as a live system, not a spreadsheet that gets updated once a quarter.

There is also a false-completeness risk: organisations sometimes treat a populated register as evidence that risks are under control, when the register only captures identified risks. Risks that are not yet known — emerging model capabilities, novel attack patterns — are by definition not in the register. The taxonomy must be reviewed proactively, not only reactively.
How It Works

An AI risk taxonomy is typically structured as a three-level hierarchy: domain (the broadest grouping), category (mid-level classification), and risk type (the specific named risk). For agentic AI, a representative taxonomy structure looks like this:
Model risks
: hallucination, factual error, output bias, demographic disparity, jailbreak, goal misgeneralisation, value misalignment, capability overhang
Agentic-specific risks
: tool misuse, excessive delegation, unintended side-effects, recursive prompt injection, memory poisoning, agent loop divergence
Operational risks
: availability failure, latency SLA breach, cost overrun, dependency failure (upstream model API), data pipeline corruption
Compliance and legal risks
: GDPR violation (PII in prompts), EU AI Act non-compliance (high-risk classification), IP infringement (training data), employment law (automated decision-making)
Security risks
: prompt injection, data exfiltration through model outputs, adversarial examples, model inversion, supply chain compromise
Reputational and ethical risks
: harmful output, discriminatory decisions, loss of human oversight, public trust erosion, dual-use misuse

Each taxonomy entry has a canonical identifier, a definition, examples, and references to applicable control categories. The risk register then references taxonomy entries by identifier rather than free text, ensuring consistency. A risk register entry has the schema: risk_id, taxonomy_id, title, description, likelihood (1-5), impact (1-5), inherent_score (L×I), control_effectiveness (1-5), residual_score, risk_owner, treatment_strategy, treatment_actions, review_date, and status.

The inherent risk score is calculated as likelihood multiplied by impact, producing a 1-25 scale. After controls are applied, the residual score reflects the remaining exposure. Risk appetite thresholds — for example, any residual score above 15 requires board-level visibility — drive escalation and reporting.
Architecture

The diagram above shows the four-category taxonomy feeding into the central register, which then drives the four-phase risk management lifecycle: identification, assessment, treatment, and monitoring. In practice, the taxonomy is versioned in a Git repository (YAML or JSON format), the register is maintained in a GRC platform or structured spreadsheet with change history, and the two artefacts are linked by taxonomy identifiers. Risk identification sessions (threat modelling workshops, post-incident reviews, horizon scanning) populate the register from the taxonomy. Treatment actions are linked to the organisation's control library and tracked in a project management tool. Monitoring triggers periodic register reviews and automated alerts when risk indicators exceed thresholds.
When to Use It

Every organisation deploying agentic AI in production contexts should maintain a risk register. The taxonomy and register are appropriate from the earliest stages of a programme, not just at launch. Establishing them during the design phase — before the first agent is deployed — ensures that risk identification is integrated into the development process rather than bolted on as a compliance exercise.

The taxonomy is especially valuable in organisations with multiple AI projects running in parallel, as it ensures that risk discoveries in one project propagate to others. A novel prompt injection vector discovered in a customer service agent should trigger a review of whether the same vector affects the internal knowledge retrieval agent.

Do not apply a heavy enterprise taxonomy to a single-team prototype that will be decommissioned in 90 days. Use a lightweight risk checklist instead. Reserve the full taxonomy and register for systems that will be in production for more than six months, handle sensitive data, or make consequential decisions.
How to Implement

Start with an existing reference taxonomy rather than building from scratch. NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001 Annex A, Microsoft's Responsible AI taxonomy, and MITRE ATLAS provide well-researched starting points. Map them to your context and add organisation-specific entries where your agent systems have unique risk profiles.
Draft the taxonomy in YAML: define each entry with id, name, domain, description, examples, and references.
Conduct a cross-functional review workshop: bring together engineering, legal, security, and product to validate coverage and add missing entries.
Version the taxonomy in Git with a changelog: tag each version with a date and a summary of what changed and why.
Seed the register: run a threat modelling session against each deployed agent system, creating register entries for every identified risk.
Score all entries: assign likelihood and impact using the 1-5 scale, justify each score with evidence or reasoning, and calculate inherent and residual scores.
Assign owners: every register entry must have a named individual owner who is responsible for treatment and monitoring, not a team or role.
Schedule reviews: high-severity risks (residual score ≥ 12) should be reviewed monthly; medium-severity quarterly; low-severity semi-annually.
Examples
Taxonomy YAML Entry

# AI Risk Taxonomy — entry example
- id: MODEL-002
  name: Hallucination
  domain: Model Risks
  category: Output Quality
  description: >
    The model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect content.
    In agentic contexts this may propagate into tool calls, decisions, or
    downstream systems without human review.
  likelihood_drivers:
    - Open-ended generation tasks with insufficient grounding
    - Retrieval failures leaving the model without factual context
    - Temperature settings above 0.7 on factual tasks
  impact_drivers:
    - Consequential decisions made on model output without verification
    - Downstream agents acting on incorrect facts
    - User trust erosion after visible errors
  examples:
    - Agent fabricates a non-existent regulatory deadline and schedules remediation work
    - Customer service bot invents a refund policy that does not exist
  references:
    - NIST AI RMF: MEASURE 2.5
    - MITRE ATLAS: AML.T0048
    - ISO 42001: Annex A.6.2
  related_controls:
    - CTRL-RAG-001  # Retrieval-augmented generation
    - CTRL-VALID-003  # Output validation pipeline
    - CTRL-HUMAN-001  # Human-in-the-loop for consequential decisions
  review_date: 2025-01-15
  version: 1.2
Risk Register JSON Entry

{
  "risk_id": "REG-AI-047",
  "taxonomy_id": "MODEL-002",
  "title": "Procurement agent hallucination on supplier terms",
  "description": "The procurement agent may fabricate supplier contract terms when
    the relevant contract is not retrieved from the document store, leading
    to purchase orders approved on incorrect terms.",
  "system": "procurement-agent-v2",
  "likelihood": 3,
  "impact": 5,
  "inherent_score": 15,
  "controls": [
    {"id": "CTRL-RAG-001", "effectiveness": 3},
    {"id": "CTRL-VALID-003", "effectiveness": 2}
  ],
  "residual_score": 9,
  "risk_owner": "alice.chen@company.com",
  "treatment_strategy": "Mitigate",
  "treatment_actions": [
    "Implement contract retrieval confidence threshold — reject below 0.8",
    "Add human review gate for orders above £10,000",
    "Deploy contract term validation against supplier master data"
  ],
  "target_residual_score": 6,
  "target_date": "2025-09-30",
  "review_date": "2025-07-01",
  "status": "Mitigating",
  "last_reviewed": "2025-04-15",
  "evidence": "JIRA-4821, control_test_results_2025Q1.pdf"
}
Real-World Example

A financial services firm deploying agentic AI for regulatory reporting faced a SOC 2 Type II audit that included a review of its AI risk programme. The audit team requested evidence of systematic risk identification, documented risk ownership, and regular review cycles. The firm had previously managed AI risks informally — engineers noted concerns in Slack threads and Jira tickets, but there was no consolidated register.

The team built a taxonomy in three weeks using NIST AI RMF as the base, adding 12 agentic-AI-specific entries derived from their internal threat model. They then ran a four-hour risk identification workshop with representatives from engineering, compliance, legal, and InfoSec, producing 34 initial register entries. By scoring each entry and assigning named owners, they reduced the critical risk count from 8 (unscored, unknown) to 3 (scored, with active treatment plans and documented evidence).

The audit produced zero findings related to risk identification. The auditors specifically noted the taxonomy cross-referencing approach as a best practice. The register also surfaced two risks that had not been previously identified — an incomplete API key rotation process for the LLM vendor and a missing data retention policy for prompt logs — both of which were addressed before the audit concluded.
Common Mistakes
Over-engineering the taxonomy
: Building a 200-entry taxonomy before anyone has used it creates a maintenance burden that kills adoption. Start with 30-50 entries, expand as real risks are identified.
Team ownership of register entries
: Assigning 'the ML team' as risk owner means no one is accountable. Every entry must have a named individual.
Confusing inherent and residual risk
: Many teams score only the risk with controls applied, losing visibility into baseline exposure. Always score both inherent (no controls) and residual (with controls) risk.
Static registers
: A register updated once at project launch then abandoned tells auditors nothing about ongoing risk management. Automate review reminders and make register updates part of the sprint cycle.
Omitting agentic-specific categories
: Adapting a general ML risk taxonomy without adding agentic-specific categories (tool misuse, recursive injection, autonomous action scope) leaves critical risks uncaptured.
Best Practices
Align taxonomy identifiers to regulatory references (NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, EU AI Act) so compliance gap analysis can be automated by joining the register to regulatory requirement tables.
Version the taxonomy in Git with semantic versioning. Breaking changes (renaming an entry, changing its identifier) increment the major version; additions increment the minor version.

Run threat modelling sessions using STRIDE adapted for AI: Spoofing (identity in multi-agent systems), Tampering (prompt injection), Repudiation (missing audit logs), Information Disclosure (PII exfiltration), Denial of Service (agent loop resource exhaustion), Elevation of Privilege (tool permission escalation).

Publish the taxonomy (without the register) to all engineering teams. Making risk vocabulary widely available ensures that risk identification is not limited to the risk team — any engineer who recognises a new risk should be able to classify it correctly.

Link register entries to control library entries bidirectionally: the register shows which controls address each risk; the control library shows which risks each control mitigates. This prevents orphaned controls that no longer address any register entry.
Performance and Scalability

A well-designed register scales to several hundred entries before requiring tooling investment. Beyond that threshold, GRC platforms (Vanta, Drata, ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) provide automation for review scheduling, owner notifications, and reporting dashboards. For organisations with multiple AI programmes running in parallel, a centralised register with programme-level views is preferable to separate registers per team — it enables cross-programme risk correlation and avoids duplicate identification effort.

The taxonomy maintenance burden scales with the pace of AI capability advancement. Organisations deploying frontier models should review the taxonomy quarterly; those on stable, version-pinned models can review semi-annually. Automated horizon-scanning feeds (MITRE ATLAS updates, NIST AI RMF advisories, security researcher disclosures) can trigger taxonomy review tickets without manual effort.
Tooling and Ecosystem

NIST AI RMF Playbook provides risk category guidance aligned with the US federal AI governance framework. MITRE ATLAS is a community-maintained knowledge base of AI-specific adversarial tactics mapped to real-world incidents. ISO 42001 Annex A lists AI management controls cross-referenceable to a risk taxonomy. Commercial GRC platforms including Vanta, Drata, and Whistic provide risk register templates with automation. For self-hosted register management, a structured YAML/JSON register in Git with a Python script to generate HTML reports is a lightweight, auditable option.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Coverage
Audit suitability
Maintenance effort
Best for
Taxonomy + Register
Systematic, complete
Excellent
Medium-high
Enterprise programmes, regulated industries
Ad-hoc issue tracking
Reactive, incomplete
Poor
Low
Prototypes, small teams
Risk checklist
Moderate (static)
Adequate
Low
Project-scoped assessments
GRC platform only (no taxonomy)
Inconsistent
Moderate
Low-medium
Organisations with existing GRC processes
Security and Reliability

The risk register itself is a sensitive document: it contains detailed information about known vulnerabilities and control gaps. Access should be restricted on a need-to-know basis. Engineering teams should see the entries relevant to their systems; the full register with all residual scores and treatment timelines should be available to risk and compliance roles, senior leadership, and auditors.

The taxonomy should be publicly accessible within the organisation — wide availability increases risk identification quality. However, the register (which describes specific gaps and treatment timelines) should be access-controlled. Store the register in a system with audit logging so that any access or modification is recorded.

Reliability of the risk management process depends on the register being a live, maintained artefact. Automated reminders to risk owners when review dates approach, monthly reports surfacing overdue entries, and quarterly programme reviews presented to the CISO or CRO are the operational mechanisms that prevent register decay.
Mental Model

Think of the taxonomy as a species classification system for risks — like biological taxonomy (kingdom, phylum, class, order, genus, species). The taxonomy gives every risk a formal, unambiguous name and places it in relation to other risks. The risk register is the field notebook: the record of every specimen actually observed in your environment, with notes on prevalence, danger level, and what you are doing about it.

Just as a naturalist would not catalogue species observations without knowing the taxonomy, and would not build a taxonomy without ever going into the field, the two artefacts are mutually dependent. The taxonomy guides where to look; the register records what is found.
Key Takeaways
An AI risk taxonomy is a controlled vocabulary for risk classification; without it, the same risk is described differently across teams and registers, making remediation and audit inconsistent.

Agentic AI requires taxonomy extensions beyond traditional ML risk: tool misuse, recursive prompt injection, autonomous action scope, and multi-agent trust boundaries are categories absent from most pre-2023 taxonomies.
The enterprise risk register operationalises the taxonomy by turning abstract categories into specific, scored, owned, and tracked risk entries with treatment plans and review schedules.
Always record both inherent score (without controls) and residual score (with controls) — the gap between the two quantifies the value delivered by the control environment.
Start from an established reference taxonomy (NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001 Annex A, MITRE ATLAS) rather than authoring from scratch, and extend it with organisation-specific entries.

The register is only as valuable as its maintenance cadence: automated review reminders, named individual owners, and regular programme reviews prevent the register from decaying into a compliance artefact with no operational value.
Linking taxonomy entries to control library entries bidirectionally enables automated gap analysis: any taxonomy entry without a corresponding control is an unmitigated risk.
